docs.title: Kairo Core
docs.text:
The Predictive Action Layer for Cloud-Native Infrastructure.

Kairo Core detects infrastructure ruptures before they cause outages — and acts on them automatically via Kubernetes, webhooks, and alerting integrations. A single Go binary, no external database required.

docs.title: Why Kairo?
docs.text: Traditional Observability Kairo Core Threshold alerts fire after the fact Rupture Index™ detects divergence hours early Rules defined per metric Adaptive ensemble learns your baseline automatically Manual incident response Tier-1 actions (scale, restart, rollback) with safety gates 5+ tools: Prom + Grafana + AM + Loki + PD One binary, one kubectl apply No reasoning about why Full XAI trace for every prediction

docs.title: Rupture Index™
docs.text:
R(t) = |α_burst(t)| / max(|α_stable(t)|, ε)
R Range State Kairo Action < 1.5 Stable / Elevated None 1.5 – 3.0 Warning Tier-3 (human) 3.0 – 5.0 Critical Tier-2 (suggested) ≥ 5.0 Emergency Tier-1 (automated)

docs.title: 8 Composite Signals
docs.text:
stress · fatigue · pressure · contagion · resilience · entropy · sentiment · healthscore

Each maps raw metrics to a single interpretable 0–1 index with published formulas.

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
=== "Kubernetes"

```bash
git clone https://github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core.git
cd kairo-core
docker build -t kairo-core:6.1.0 .
kubectl apply -f deploy/
kubectl port-forward svc/kairo-core 8080:8080 -n kairo-system
curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health
```

=== "Docker"

```bash
docker run -d \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -v kairo-data:/var/lib/kairo \
  -e KAIRO_JWT_SECRET=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \
  kairo-core:6.1.0

curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health
```

=== "Helm"

```bash
helm install kairo-core ./helm \
  --namespace kairo-system \
  --create-namespace \
  --set auth.jwtSecret=$(openssl rand -hex 32)
```

docs.title: About
docs.text: Kairo Core is a predictive failure detection engine for cloud-native infrastructure, built by Selim Benfradj.

docs.title: Origins
docs.text:
Kairo Core is the v6.0 clean-room rewrite of the Observability Holistic Engine (OHE), a project that began in 2025 with a core insight: existing observability tools answer "What is broken?" — none answer "When will it break, and why?"

OHE v5.0 proved the thesis with a single Go binary delivering dual-scale CA-ILR predictions, dissipative fatigue formulas, and the METRICS.md explainability standard. Kairo Core (v6.0+) extends this into a production-grade platform with a Kubernetes operator, gRPC ingest, eventbus integration, and official SDKs.

docs.title: Philosophy
docs.text:
Prevention is better than cure.

Three principles that have been non-negotiable since v4.0:

  1. Transparent AI — every prediction traceable to a published formula. No black boxes.
  2. Sovereign deployment — a single static binary, no external database, runs on a Raspberry Pi 4.
  3. Auditable by design — KPI formulas are versioned release artifacts. CISOs, auditors, and SREs can challenge any decision.

docs.title: The Problem
docs.text:
Current observability solutions split along two failing axes:

  • Open-source stacks (Prometheus + Grafana + Loki) demand 5+ services, 8 GB+ RAM, and weeks of integration. They answer "What is broken?" via static thresholds — never "When will it break?"
  • Enterprise SaaS (Datadog, Dynatrace) provide black-box AI at prohibitive cost with opaque decision logic.

Neither predicts. Neither explains.

docs.title: The Kairo Approach
docs.text: Kairo treats infrastructure as a living organism — measuring vital signs, behaviours, stress responses, and social dynamics through auditable composite KPIs.

docs.title: Rupture Index™ — the core innovation
docs.text:
R(t) = |α_burst(t)| / max(|α_stable(t)|, ε)

Two Incremental Linear Regression (ILR) windows run in parallel per metric:

Window Captures 60 min (ILR_stable) Long-term baseline — what is normal? 5 min (ILR_burst) Short-term acceleration — is something happening right now?

When R > 3, a metric is accelerating 3× faster than its own baseline — a memory leak, cascade failure, or saturation event in progress. Kairo detects this before the metric reaches 80% saturation.

docs.title: Why not LSTM?
docs.text:
Model MAE RAM Inference Efficiency Score LSTM 2.0% 200+ MB 500 ms < 0.0001 ARIMA 4.1% 85 MB 210 ms 0.0001 ILR (Kairo) 6.2% 0.5 MB 0.8 ms 1,550×

ILR trades +2.1% MAE for 170× less RAM and 262× faster inference. 1,550× more efficient than ARIMA — validated on a Raspberry Pi 4 over 40,320 samples.

docs.title: 8 Composite Signals
docs.text:
Kairo fuses raw telemetry into 8 interpretable signals with published formulas:

Signal Formula Example state stress 0.3·CPU + 0.2·RAM + 0.2·Latency + 0.2·Errors + 0.1·Timeouts 0.72 → "Stressed" fatigue max(0, F_prev + (stress − 0.3) − λ) 0.45 → "Tired" pressure d(stress̄)/dt + ∫errors 0.15 → stable contagion Σ E_ij × D_ij 0.05 → isolated healthscore (1−stress)(1−fatigue)(1−pressure)(1−contagion)×100 43 → needs attention

docs.title: Adaptive Ensemble (v6.1)
docs.text: Five models weighted by online MAE over a 1-hour window: CA-ILR, ARIMA, Holt-Winters, MAD, EWMA. Weights update every 60 seconds. No manual tuning. No profile configuration.

docs.title: Benchmarks
docs.text: Criterion Prom/Grafana/Loki Datadog Kairo Core RAM idle ~450 MB ~180 MB 22 MB Setup time ~30 min ~5 min < 1 min Prediction accuracy ❌ None ✅ Black-box ✅ Transparent, 6.2% MAE False positives (backup spikes) ❌ Yes ⚠️ Sometimes ✅ No (λ dissipation) Exponential crash detection ❌ No ✅ Black-box ✅ R > 3 (auditable) Air-gapped ready ⚠️ Complex ❌ Impossible ✅ Native Efficiency score 1× ~0.0001× 1,550×

docs.title: API
docs.text: Kairo Core exposes a REST API v2 with 44 endpoints. All endpoints are under /api/v2/.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
Two auth methods are supported:

=== "JWT Bearer"

```bash
# Login to get a token
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v2/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username":"admin","password":"<jwt_secret>"}'

# Use the token
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health
```

=== "API Key"

```bash
# Create a key (requires JWT auth)
curl -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v2/apikeys \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" \
  -d '{"name":"ci-pipeline","scopes":["read","write"]}'

# Use the key (ohe_* format)
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer ohe_abc123" http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health
```

docs.title: Response envelope
docs.text:
All responses follow a consistent JSON envelope:

{
  "success": true,
  "data": { ... },
  "timestamp": "2026-04-28T10:00:00Z"
}

Errors:

{
  "success": false,
  "error": {
    "code": "UNAUTHORIZED",
    "message": "API key expired"
  },
  "timestamp": "2026-04-28T10:00:00Z"
}

docs.title: Fusion Engine
docs.text: The Fusion Engine is the analytical core of Kairo. It combines normalised telemetry from all three pipelines into 8 composite signals, runs the adaptive ensemble, and produces the Rupture Index™ for every tracked host.

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
1. Composite signal computation — recalculate all 8 signals on every new data point
  2. Adaptive ensemble — maintain per-host model weights and update them every 60 s
  3. Rupture detection — compute R = |α_burst| / |α_stable| and compare against thresholds
  4. XAI trace — record which signals and models contributed to each prediction

docs.title: Signal computation order
docs.text:
Raw metrics → stress → fatigue → pressure
Raw errors  → contagion
stress + uptime + throughput → sentiment
stress + variance → entropy
historical slope → resilience
all 4 primary signals → healthscore

Signals are computed in dependency order: stress must be ready before fatigue and pressure.

docs.title: Adaptive ensemble inside the fusion engine
docs.text:
type EnsembleState struct {
    Weights map[string]float64  // ca_ilr, arima, holt_winters, mad, ewma
    Errors  map[string]float64  // sliding 1-hour MAE per model
    Updated time.Time
}

Every 60 seconds, the fusion engine:

  1. Collects actual vs predicted values for each model over the past 1 hour
  2. Computes MAE per model: MAE_i = mean(|actual − predicted_i|)
  3. Normalises inverse MAE to produce new weights
  4. Stores updated weights in EnsembleState

The final prediction passed to the rupture detector is the weighted sum:

prediction(t) = Σ_i weight_i(t) × prediction_i(t)

docs.title: Rupture detector
docs.text:
type RuptureDetector struct {
    stableILR *ILR   // 60-min window
    burstILR  *ILR   // 5-min window
}

func (d *RuptureDetector) R() float64 {
    denom := math.Abs(d.stableILR.Alpha)
    if denom < 1e-6 {
        denom = 1e-6
    }
    return math.Abs(d.burstILR.Alpha) / denom
}

docs.title: Storage
docs.text:
The fusion engine writes composite signal values to BadgerDB with tiered compaction:

Granularity Retention Use 15 s 7 days Raw metrics 1 min 30 days Aggregated metrics + logs 5 min 1 year KPI history 1 h 400 days Long-term compliance

docs.title: XAI — explainability trace
docs.text:
For every rupture, the fusion engine records:

{
  "rupture_id": "r_abc123",
  "host": "web-01",
  "rupture_index": 4.2,
  "model_contributions": {
    "ca_ilr":       { "weight": 0.35, "prediction": 4.4 },
    "arima":        { "weight": 0.22, "prediction": 4.0 },
    "holt_winters": { "weight": 0.18, "prediction": 3.9 },
    "mad":          { "weight": 0.14, "prediction": 4.5 },
    "ewma":         { "weight": 0.11, "prediction": 4.1 }
  },
  "dominant_signal": "stress",
  "signal_values": {
    "stress": 0.72, "fatigue": 0.41, "pressure": 0.35,
    "contagion": 0.12, "healthscore": 43.2

Retrieve it at GET /api/v2/explain/{rupture_id}.

docs.title: Kubernetes Operator
docs.text: Kairo v6.1 ships a Kubernetes operator that manages KairoInstance custom resources. The operator reconciles a Deployment, Service, and PersistentVolumeClaim for each instance, keeping the cluster state aligned with the declared spec.

docs.title: CRD: KairoInstance
docs.text:
apiVersion: kairo.io/v1alpha1
kind: KairoInstance
metadata:
  name: production
  namespace: kairo-system
spec:
  image: kairo-core:6.1.0        # container image to run
  port: 8080                      # HTTP port (REST API)
  storageSize: 20Gi               # PVC size for BadgerDB
  apiKey:
    secretRef: kairo-api-key      # K8s Secret containing the API key
  replicas: 1                     # number of replicas (default: 1)

docs.title: Resources created
docs.text:
For each KairoInstance, the operator creates:

Resource Name Purpose Deployment {name} Runs the kairo-core container Service {name} ClusterIP on the specified port PersistentVolumeClaim {name}-storage BadgerDB data directory

docs.title: Deploy the operator
docs.text:
# Apply CRD + RBAC + operator Deployment
kubectl apply -f deploy/operator/

# Verify the operator is running
kubectl get pods -n kairo-system -l app=kairo-operator

# Create a KairoInstance
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: kairo.io/v1alpha1
kind: KairoInstance
metadata:
  name: production
  namespace: kairo-system
spec:
  image: kairo-core:6.1.0
  port: 8080
  storageSize: 20Gi
  apiKey:
    secretRef: kairo-api-key
EOF

docs.title: Reconciliation loop
docs.text:
The operator uses controller-runtime and follows the standard Kubernetes reconcile pattern:

  1. Observe — read current cluster state for the KairoInstance
  2. Diff — compare with desired spec
  3. Act — create / update / delete owned resources
  4. Status — update KairoInstanceStatus with ready and message
type KairoInstanceStatus struct {
    Ready   bool      `json:"ready"`
    Message string    `json:"message"`
    Updated time.Time `json:"updated"`
}

Check status:

kubectl get kairoinstance production -o jsonpath='{.status}' | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: RBAC requirements
docs.text:
The operator service account requires:

rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["services", "persistentvolumeclaims"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
  - apiGroups: ["kairo.io"]
    resources: ["kairoinstances", "kairoinstances/status"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update", "patch"]

docs.title: Multiple instances
docs.text:
You can run multiple KairoInstance resources in different namespaces for tenant isolation:

kubectl apply -f production-instance.yaml   # namespace: production
kubectl apply -f staging-instance.yaml      # namespace: staging

Each instance manages its own independent BadgerDB storage and API key.

docs.title: Pipelines
docs.text: Kairo runs three independent ingestion pipelines — metrics, logs, and traces — that feed a shared fusion engine.

docs.title: Pipeline flow
docs.text:
graph LR
    A[Ingest<br/>gRPC · OTLP · Prom rw · DogSD] --> B[Normalise<br/>downsample · [0,1] scale]
    B --> C[Aggregate<br/>per-host windows]
    C --> D[Fusion Engine<br/>composites + ensemble]
    D --> E[Rupture Detector]
    E --> F[Actions + API]

docs.title: Metric pipeline
docs.text:
Receives metrics from any of:

Source Endpoint / Port Prometheus remote_write POST /api/v2/write OTLP/HTTP POST /api/v2/v1/metrics gRPC (v6.1) :9090 DogStatsD UDP :8125

Processing steps:

  1. Parse — decode protobuf (Prometheus), OTLP proto, or StatsD format
  2. Normalise — scale all values to [0, 1] per metric type
  3. Downsample — reduce to 15-second granularity for ILR inputs
  4. Window — maintain rolling 5-min (burst) and 60-min (stable) windows per host

docs.title: Log pipeline
docs.text:
Receives logs from:

Source Endpoint OTLP/HTTP POST /api/v2/v1/logs REST push POST /api/v2/logs

Logs feed the entropy and sentiment composite signals. Error-rate extraction drives the contagion signal.

docs.title: Trace pipeline
docs.text:
Receives distributed traces from:

Source Endpoint OTLP/HTTP POST /api/v2/v1/traces

Span error rates and latency percentiles feed the stress and pressure signals.

docs.title: Back-pressure (gRPC ingest)
docs.text:
The gRPC ingest server (v6.1) enforces:

  • Max message size: 4 MB
  • Back-pressure: if the internal metric queue is full, the server returns RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED

This prevents a metrics storm from overwhelming the fusion engine.

docs.title: Eventbus integration (v6.1)
docs.text:
When eventbus.driver is set to nats or kafka, every processed rupture event is published in addition to being stored:

kairo.rupture.{host}  →  published on every R state change
kairo.actions.tier1   →  published when a Tier-1 action fires

Configure in kairo.yaml:

eventbus:
  driver: kafka
  kafka:
    brokers: ["kafka:9092"]
    topic: kairo.events

docs.title: Test coverage requirement
docs.text:
All packages must maintain ≥ 80% test coverage. The CI gate fails below this threshold.

# Check coverage for a specific package
go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./internal/pipeline/metrics/...
go tool cover -func=coverage.out

docs.title: Reporting bugs
docs.text:
Open an issue on GitHub with:

  1. Kairo version (kairo-core --version)
  2. kairo.yaml (sanitised — remove secrets)
  3. Relevant logs
  4. Steps to reproduce

docs.title: Proposing features
docs.text:
Open a GitHub Discussion under the Ideas category. Include:

  • Problem statement
  • Proposed solution
  • Alternatives considered

Large features (new ingest protocol, new composite signal, new action provider) benefit from a short design doc before implementation.

docs.title: Code style
docs.text:
• Go 1.18 — no generics (any, ~, type parameters), no log/slog, no errors.Join
  • Immutable data patterns — never mutate a struct in-place, return new copies
  • No comments explaining what the code does — only why (non-obvious constraints)
  • Files ≤ 800 lines, functions ≤ 50 lines
  • Module path: github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text: Kairo Core is built around three layered ideas: predict, explain, act.

docs.title: Core model
docs.text:
Raw metrics / logs / traces
        │
        ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │  Composite      │  8 signals: stress, fatigue, pressure, contagion,
  │  Signals        │  resilience, entropy, sentiment, healthscore
  └────────┬────────┘
           │
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │  Adaptive       │  CA-ILR × ARIMA × Holt-Winters × MAD × EWMA
  │  Ensemble       │  Online MAE-based weight adaptation (v6.1)
  └────────┬────────┘
           │
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │  Rupture        │  R = |α_burst| / |α_stable|
  │  Index™         │  R ≥ 3.0 → Warning  /  R ≥ 5.0 → Emergency
  └────────┬────────┘
           │
           ▼
  ┌─────────────────┐
  │  Action         │  Tier-1 auto / Tier-2 suggested / Tier-3 human
  │  Engine         │  K8s · Webhook · Alertmanager · PagerDuty
  └─────────────────┘

docs.title: Concept pages
docs.text: Page What it covers Rupture Index™ The core prediction metric — dual-scale CA-ILR maths Composite Signals The 8 interpretable signals and their formulas Surge Profiles / Adaptive Ensemble How model weights adapt online to your traffic patterns Action Engine Tier system, safety gates, supported integrations

docs.title: Action Engine
docs.text: The Kairo Action Engine translates rupture detections into concrete remediation steps. It supports three execution tiers, four integration targets, and a suite of safety gates to prevent runaway automation.

docs.title: Execution tiers
docs.text:
Tier Mode Trigger Who acts Tier-1 Automatic R ≥ 5.0 + confidence ≥ 0.85 Kairo (no human needed) Tier-2 Suggested R ≥ 3.0 + confidence ≥ 0.60 Human approves via API Tier-3 Alert only R ≥ 1.5 Human decides

Configure the execution mode in kairo.yaml:

actions:
  execution_mode: suggest   # shadow | suggest | auto

docs.title: Kubernetes
docs.text:
# Available K8s actions
- scale:    increase replica count on target Deployment
- restart:  rolling restart of target Deployment
- cordon:   mark Node unschedulable
- drain:    evict all Pods from a Node
- isolate:  apply NetworkPolicy to block ingress/egress

docs.title: Webhook
docs.text: Send an HTTP POST to any URL with the rupture payload. Useful for triggering CI/CD pipelines, Slack notifications, or custom scripts.

docs.title: Alertmanager
docs.text: Raise or resolve alerts in Prometheus Alertmanager. Kairo generates compatible alert payloads with labels, annotations, and generatorURL.

docs.title: PagerDuty
docs.text: Create or update PagerDuty incidents with severity, rupture context, and XAI explanation link.

docs.title: Safety gates
docs.text:
Kairo enforces multiple safety gates before executing any Tier-1 action:

Gate Default Description Rate limit 6 / hour Max Tier-1 actions per target per hour Cooldown 300 s Minimum gap between two actions on the same target Namespace allowlist [] (all blocked) Only act on pods in listed namespaces Confidence threshold 0.85 Ensemble confidence required for auto-execution Emergency stop off POST /api/v2/actions/emergency-stop halts all Tier-1 actions globally

Configuration:

actions:
  execution_mode: auto
  safety:
    rate_limit_per_hour: 6
    cooldown_seconds: 300
    namespace_allowlist:
      - production
      - staging

docs.title: Action lifecycle
docs.text:
Rupture detected (R ≥ threshold)
        │
        ▼
Safety gates evaluated
        │
   ┌────┴────┐
  Pass      Fail → Log + skip
   │
   ▼
execution_mode?
   ├── shadow  → Log action, do nothing
   ├── suggest → POST to /api/v2/actions (pending approval)
   └── auto    → Execute immediately (Tier-1) or queue (Tier-2)
        │
        ▼
Emit event: kairo.actions.tier1 (eventbus)
Emit metric: kairo_actions_total{tier="1",result="ok"}

docs.title: Approving a suggested action
docs.text:
# List pending actions
GET /api/v2/actions

# Approve a specific action
POST /api/v2/actions/{id}/approve

# Emergency stop all Tier-1 auto-actions
POST /api/v2/actions/emergency-stop

docs.title: Eventbus events
docs.text:
When an eventbus is configured (nats or kafka), every Tier-1 action publishes:

kairo.actions.tier1   → { action_id, host, type, params, rupture_id, timestamp }
kairo.rupture.{host}  → on every rupture state change

docs.title: Composite Signals
docs.text: Kairo computes 8 composite signals from raw telemetry. Each maps multiple input metrics to a single 0–1 interpretable index with a published formula. No black boxes.

docs.title: Signal overview
docs.text: Signal Range What it measures stress 0–1 Instantaneous load pressure across CPU, RAM, latency, errors fatigue 0–1 Accumulated stress over time (dissipative — recovers during idle) pressure 0–1 Rate of change in stress + integrated error load contagion 0–1 Error propagation speed across service dependencies resilience 0–1 Inverse of failure probability — how quickly does the system recover? entropy 0–1 Configuration drift and unpredictability in system behaviour sentiment 0–1 Mood proxy: uptime × throughput vs errors × restarts healthscore 0–100 Product signal: (1-stress) × (1-fatigue) × (1-pressure) × (1-contagion) × 100

docs.title: Stress
docs.text:
stress(t) = 0.3·CPU(t) + 0.2·RAM(t) + 0.2·Latency(t) + 0.2·Errors(t) + 0.1·Timeouts(t)

All inputs normalised to [0, 1].

stress State < 0.3 Calm 0.3 – 0.6 Nervous 0.6 – 0.8 Stressed ≥ 0.8 Panic

docs.title: Fatigue (dissipative)
docs.text:
F(t) = max(0,  F(t−1) + (stress(t) − R_threshold) − λ)

  R_threshold = 0.3  (rest threshold)
  λ           = 0.05 (healing rate per interval)

The dissipative term λ prevents false fatigue alarms from legitimate scheduled spikes (nightly backups, batch jobs). The system "heals" during low-stress periods.

fatigue State Recommended action < 0.3 Rested Normal monitoring 0.3 – 0.6 Tired Increase observation frequency 0.6 – 0.8 Exhausted Plan maintenance window ≥ 0.8 Burnout imminent Preventive restart

docs.title: Pressure
docs.text:
pressure(t) = d(stress̄)/dt + ∫₀ᵗ errors̄(τ) dτ
pressure Interpretation > 0.1 sustained for 10 min Storm likely in ~2 hours Stable Steady-state conditions < 0 System recovering

docs.title: Contagion
docs.text:
contagion(t) = Σ_{i,j} E_{ij}(t) × D_{ij}

Where E_ij = error rate from service i to j, D_ij = dependency weight (0–1).

contagion State Action < 0.3 Isolated Normal 0.3 – 0.6 Spreading Monitor closely 0.6 – 0.8 Epidemic Isolate affected services ≥ 0.8 Pandemic Global incident response

docs.title: Sentiment
docs.text:
sentiment(t) = (Uptime(t) × Throughput(t)) / (Errors(t) × Timeouts(t) × Restarts(t) + ε)

A high sentiment means the service is happy and performant. Near zero indicates degraded user experience.

docs.title: Health Score
docs.text:
healthscore = (1 − stress) × (1 − fatigue) × (1 − pressure) × (1 − contagion) × 100

A single 0–100 operational dashboard number. Below 60 indicates a service needing attention.

docs.title: API
docs.text:
# Query any signal for a host
GET /api/v2/kpi/{signal}/{host}

# All signals for a host
GET /api/v2/kpi/healthscore/web-01
GET /api/v2/kpi/stress/web-01
GET /api/v2/kpi/fatigue/web-01

Example response:

{
  "signal": "stress",
  "host": "web-01",
  "value": 0.72,
  "state": "Stressed",
  "trend": "up",
  "timestamp": "2026-04-28T10:00:00Z"
}

docs.title: Prometheus self-metrics
docs.text:
Kairo exports all 8 signals as Prometheus metrics:

kairo_kpi_healthscore{host="web-01"} 61.4
kairo_kpi_stress{host="web-01"} 0.72
kairo_kpi_fatigue{host="web-01"} 0.41
# ... (one series per signal per host)

Scrape at GET /api/v2/metrics.

docs.title: Rupture Index™
docs.text: The Rupture Index™ (R) is Kairo's core predictive metric. It measures the ratio of short-term slope acceleration to the long-term baseline slope across every tracked metric.

docs.title: Formula
docs.text:
R(t) = |α_burst(t)| / max(|α_stable(t)|, ε)

  α_burst  = slope from the 5-min CA-ILR window  (captures sudden change)
  α_stable = slope from the 60-min CA-ILR window  (captures the baseline)
  ε        = 1e-6  (numerical stability guard)

When a metric is accelerating far faster than its baseline — a hallmark of memory leaks, cascade failures, and saturation events — R spikes above 1. Kairo raises actions when R crosses the configured thresholds.

docs.title: Threshold table
docs.text:
R Range State Default Action < 1.0 Stable None 1.0 – 1.5 Elevated None 1.5 – 3.0 Warning Tier-3 (human alert) 3.0 – 5.0 Critical Tier-2 (suggested action) ≥ 5.0 Emergency Tier-1 (automated action)

Thresholds are configurable via predictor.rupture_threshold in kairo.yaml.

docs.title: Dual-scale CA-ILR engine
docs.text:
Kairo maintains two Incremental Linear Regression (ILR) instances per metric:

Instance Window Purpose ILR_stable 60 min (240 samples @ 15 s) Long-term baseline trend ILR_burst 5 min (20 samples @ 15 s) Micro-acceleration, crisis onset

Each ILR runs Welford-style O(1) incremental updates — no history stored, constant memory:

μx(n+1) = μx(n) + (x_{n+1} − μx(n)) / (n+1)
C_xy    = C_xy + (x_{n+1} − μx(n)) · (y_{n+1} − μy(n+1))
α       = C_xy / V_x    (slope)
β       = μy − α · μx   (intercept)

docs.title: Why not LSTM or ARIMA?
docs.text:
Model MAE RAM Inference Efficiency LSTM 2.0% 200+ MB 500 ms < 0.0001 ARIMA 4.1% 85 MB 210 ms 0.0001 ILR (Kairo) 6.2% 0.5 MB 0.8 ms 1,550×

ILR trades +2.1% MAE vs ARIMA for 170× less RAM and 262× faster inference. Validated over 40,320 samples on a Raspberry Pi 4 — Kairo runs on edge hardware.

docs.title: XAI — explaining a rupture
docs.text:
Every rupture has a traceable explanation endpoint:

curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/explain/<rupture_id>

{
  "rupture_id": "r_abc123",
  "host": "web-01",
  "rupture_index": 4.2,
  "state": "critical",
  "dominant_signal": "stress",
  "alpha_burst": 0.042,
  "alpha_stable": 0.010,
  "formula": "R = |α_burst| / |α_stable| = 0.042 / 0.010 = 4.2",
  "recommendation": "CPU stress is the primary driver — consider horizontal scaling"
}

docs.title: API
docs.text:
# Current rupture index for a host
GET /api/v2/rupture/{host}

# All active ruptures
GET /api/v2/ruptures

# Formula breakdown
GET /api/v2/explain/{rupture_id}/formula

docs.title: Adaptive Ensemble (Surge Profiles)
docs.text: Kairo v6.1 introduces adaptive ensemble weighting — the engine continuously learns which prediction model fits your infrastructure's traffic patterns best, and shifts weight toward it automatically.

docs.title: The five models
docs.text: Model Strengths Weaknesses CA-ILR (Dual-scale) O(1) update, detects acceleration, edge-native Linear assumption within window ARIMA Strong on stationary series with trends Computationally heavier, assumes linearity Holt-Winters Excellent on seasonal / periodic patterns Requires clear seasonality MAD (Median Absolute Deviation) Robust to outliers No temporal memory EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) Smooth, reacts to recent data Slow to detect sudden shifts

docs.title: Weight adaptation algorithm
docs.text:
Every 60 seconds, Kairo evaluates prediction accuracy over the past 1-hour sliding window:

error_model_i = MAE(predicted_i, actual) over last 1 hour

weight_i = (1 / error_i) / Σ_j (1 / error_j)

Models with lower recent prediction error receive higher weight. Weights are normalised to sum to 1.0.

The final ensemble prediction is:

prediction(t) = Σ_i weight_i(t) × prediction_i(t)

docs.title: Enable adaptive weighting
docs.text:
# kairo.yaml
ensemble:
  adaptive: true

When false (default), all five models are weighted equally at 0.20 each.

docs.title: Monitoring weights via API
docs.text:
GET /api/v2/ensemble/{host}

{
  "host": "web-01",
  "updated_at": "2026-04-28T10:00:00Z",
  "weights": {
    "ca_ilr":       0.35,
    "arima":        0.22,
    "holt_winters": 0.18,
    "mad":          0.14,
    "ewma":         0.11
  }
}

docs.title: Surge profiles — how Kairo handles load spikes
docs.text:
Kairo does not require you to pre-define "surge profiles" or maintenance windows. The dissipative fatigue formula (λ healing) and the adaptive ensemble work together to avoid false alarms during planned load events:

  1. At spike onset — ILR_burst slope increases, R rises.
  2. If the spike is brief — fatigue dissipates (λ recovery), R falls back.
  3. If the spike sustains — R stays elevated, ensemble weights shift toward models that track the new pattern.
  4. Weights rebalance — once the spike ends, MAE improves for baseline-tracking models and they regain weight.

This means a nightly backup job gradually "teaches" the ensemble that high CPU at 02:00 UTC is expected — no manual intervention required.

docs.title: Prometheus self-metrics for ensemble
docs.text:
kairo_ensemble_weight{host="web-01",model="ca_ilr"}       0.35
kairo_ensemble_weight{host="web-01",model="arima"}         0.22
kairo_ensemble_weight{host="web-01",model="holt_winters"}  0.18
kairo_ensemble_weight{host="web-01",model="mad"}           0.14
kairo_ensemble_weight{host="web-01",model="ewma"}          0.11

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Get Kairo Core up and running in minutes.

docs.title: Installation paths
docs.text: Method Best for Kubernetes / Helm Production, GitOps workflows Docker Local dev, single-host deployments Binary Edge, air-gapped, minimal footprint

docs.title: Recommended flow
docs.text:
1. Install → — choose Docker, Kubernetes, or binary
  2. Quickstart → — ingest your first metrics and see the Rupture Index in 5 minutes
  3. Configuration → — tune kairo.yaml for your environment

docs.title: Minimum requirements
docs.text: Resource Minimum Recommended CPU 0.5 core 1 core RAM 64 MB 256 MB Disk 1 GB 20 GB (400-day retention) Go 1.18+ — (build only) Kubernetes 1.24+ — (operator only)

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Kairo is configured via kairo.yaml. Pass the path with --config.

docs.title: Full reference
docs.text:
# kairo.yaml

mode: connected          # connected | stateless | shadow

ingest:
  http_port: 8080        # REST API + Prometheus remote_write
  grpc_port: 9090        # gRPC ingest (v6.1)

eventbus:
  driver: none           # none | nats | kafka
  # nats:
  #   url: "nats://localhost:4222"
  #   stream: kairo-events
  # kafka:
  #   brokers: ["localhost:9092"]
  #   topic: kairo.events

ensemble:
  adaptive: false        # true = online MAE-based weight adaptation (v6.1)
  # weights are updated every 60s over a 1-hour sliding window when adaptive: true

predictor:
  rupture_threshold: 3.0          # R ≥ threshold → Warning
  confidence_thresholds:
    auto_action: 0.85             # confidence required for Tier-1 auto-action
    alert: 0.60                   # confidence required to raise an alert

actions:
  execution_mode: shadow          # shadow | suggest | auto
  safety:
    rate_limit_per_hour: 6        # max Tier-1 actions per target per hour
    cooldown_seconds: 300         # min gap between actions on the same target
    namespace_allowlist:          # only act on pods in these namespaces
      - production
      - staging

auth:
  jwt_secret: ""                  # required — set via env KAIRO_JWT_SECRET
  api_keys: []                    # pre-provisioned keys (ohe_* format)

storage:
  path: /var/lib/kairo            # BadgerDB data directory
  retention:
    raw_metrics_days: 7
    logs_days: 30
    kpis_days: 400                # compliance-ready long-term KPI retention

docs.title: mode
docs.text: Value Behaviour connected Full rupture detection, actions, and XAI stateless No persistence — useful for testing ingest shadow Actions computed but never executed (dry-run)

docs.title: actions.execution_mode
docs.text: Value Behaviour shadow Actions logged only — nothing executed suggest Actions POSTed as suggestions, wait for /approve auto Tier-1 actions executed automatically with safety gates

docs.title: ensemble.adaptive
docs.text:
When true, Kairo computes per-model MAE over a 1-hour sliding window and normalises weights every 60 seconds. Models with lower error receive more weight. The five models are: CA-ILR, ARIMA, Holt-Winters, MAD, EWMA.

When false, equal weights are used (default: 0.20 each).

docs.title: Environment variables
docs.text:
Every YAML key can be overridden with an environment variable using KAIRO_ prefix and _-separated key path:

Env var Equivalent YAML KAIRO_JWT_SECRET auth.jwt_secret KAIRO_INGEST_HTTP_PORT ingest.http_port KAIRO_INGEST_GRPC_PORT ingest.grpc_port KAIRO_ACTIONS_EXECUTION_MODE actions.execution_mode KAIRO_ENSEMBLE_ADAPTIVE ensemble.adaptive KAIRO_STORAGE_PATH storage.path

docs.title: Minimal production config
docs.text:
mode: connected
auth:
  jwt_secret: "${KAIRO_JWT_SECRET}"
actions:
  execution_mode: suggest
  safety:
    rate_limit_per_hour: 6
    namespace_allowlist:
      - production
storage:
  path: /var/lib/kairo

docs.title: Kubernetes
docs.text: The recommended production deployment uses the bundled manifests or Helm chart.

docs.title: Using kubectl
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core.git
cd kairo-core

# Build the image (or use a pre-built tag)
docker build -t kairo-core:6.1.0 .

# Create namespace + deploy
kubectl apply -f deploy/

# Verify pods are running
kubectl get pods -n kairo-system

# Port-forward to test locally
kubectl port-forward svc/kairo-core 8080:8080 -n kairo-system
curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health

docs.title: Using Helm
docs.text:
helm install kairo-core ./helm \
  --namespace kairo-system \
  --create-namespace \
  --set auth.jwtSecret=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \
  --set storage.size=20Gi

To upgrade:

helm upgrade kairo-core ./helm --namespace kairo-system

docs.title: Using the KairoInstance CRD (Operator)
docs.text:
If you have the Kairo Operator installed, deploy a full instance declaratively:

apiVersion: kairo.io/v1alpha1
kind: KairoInstance
metadata:
  name: production
  namespace: kairo-system
spec:
  image: kairo-core:6.1.0
  port: 8080
  storageSize: 20Gi
  apiKey:
    secretRef: kairo-api-key
  replicas: 1

kubectl apply -f kairo-instance.yaml

See Operator → for full CRD reference.

docs.title: Docker
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  --name kairo \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -p 9090:9090 \
  -v kairo-data:/var/lib/kairo \
  -e KAIRO_JWT_SECRET=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \
  kairo-core:6.1.0
Port Protocol Purpose 8080 HTTP REST API v2, Prometheus metrics 9090 gRPC gRPC ingest (v6.1)

Verify:

curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health
# {"status":"ok","rupture_detection":"active"}

docs.title: Binary
docs.text:
Download the latest release binary and run it directly:

# Linux amd64
curl -fsSL https://github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core/releases/latest/download/kairo-linux-amd64 \
  -o /usr/local/bin/kairo-core
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/kairo-core

kairo-core --config=/etc/kairo/kairo.yaml

Kairo ships as a single static binary — no runtime dependencies, no external database.

docs.title: Build from Source
docs.text:
Requires Go 1.18+:

git clone https://github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core.git
cd kairo-core/workdir
go build -o kairo-core ./cmd/kairo-core
./kairo-core --config=configs/kairo.yaml

Run tests:

go test -race -timeout=120s ./...
go test -coverprofile=coverage.out ./... && go tool cover -func=coverage.out | grep total

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get Kairo running and see the Rupture Index in under 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1 — Start Kairo
docs.text:
docker run -d \
  --name kairo \
  -p 8080:8080 \
  -v kairo-data:/var/lib/kairo \
  -e KAIRO_JWT_SECRET=dev-secret-change-in-prod \
  kairo-core:6.1.0

docs.title: Step 2 — Verify health
docs.text:
curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health

Expected response:

{"status":"ok","rupture_detection":"active","uptime_seconds":3}

docs.title: Step 3 — Create an API key
docs.text:
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v2/auth/login \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"username":"admin","password":"dev-secret-change-in-prod"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool

Copy the returned JWT or use X-API-Key after creating a key via /api/v2/apikeys.

docs.title: Step 4 — Send metrics (Prometheus remote_write)
docs.text:
# Push a sample metric payload
curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8080/api/v2/write \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer <your-token>" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-protobuf" \
  --data-binary @sample.prw

Or configure your Prometheus to remote_write to Kairo:

# prometheus.yml
remote_write:
  - url: http://kairo-core:8080/api/v2/write
    basic_auth:
      password: <your-api-key>

docs.title: Step 5 — Query the Rupture Index
docs.text:
API_KEY=<your-api-key>

# Get rupture index for a host
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v2/rupture/web-01 | python3 -m json.tool

Sample response:

{
  "host": "web-01",
  "rupture_index": 1.2,
  "state": "elevated",
  "time_to_failure_seconds": null,
  "dominant_signal": "stress"
}

docs.title: Step 6 — Query composite signals
docs.text:
# healthscore (0–100 product of stress, fatigue, pressure, contagion)
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \
  "http://localhost:8080/api/v2/kpi/healthscore/web-01"

# All 8 signals
for sig in stress fatigue pressure contagion resilience entropy sentiment healthscore; do
  echo -n "$sig: "
  curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \
    "http://localhost:8080/api/v2/kpi/$sig/web-01" | python3 -c "import sys,json; d=json.load(sys.stdin); print(d.get('value','?'))"
done

docs.title: Step 7 — Explain a prediction
docs.text:
curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $API_KEY" \
  http://localhost:8080/api/v2/explain/<rupture_id> | python3 -m json.tool

This returns the formula, contributing signals, and recommended action.

docs.title: Operations
docs.text:
Guides for running Kairo Core in production.

Guide What it covers Deployment Kubernetes, Helm, Docker, bare-metal production configs Self Monitoring Prometheus metrics Kairo exports about itself Troubleshooting Common failure modes and how to resolve them

docs.title: Production checklist
docs.text:
• [ ] auth.jwt_secret set via environment variable, not hardcoded in config
  • [ ] actions.execution_mode set to suggest or auto (not shadow) when ready
  • [ ] actions.safety.namespace_allowlist populated with production namespaces
  • [ ] storage.path backed by a persistent volume (PVC in K8s)
  • [ ] Scrape GET /api/v2/metrics in Prometheus for Kairo self-metrics
  • [ ] Alert on kairo_rupture_index > 3.0 in your Alertmanager
  • [ ] Emergency stop documented in your runbook: POST /api/v2/actions/emergency-stop

docs.title: Kubernetes with Helm (recommended)
docs.text:
helm install kairo-core ./helm \
  --namespace kairo-system \
  --create-namespace \
  --set auth.jwtSecret=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \
  --set storage.size=50Gi \
  --set actions.executionMode=suggest \
  --set actions.safety.namespaceAllowlist="{production,staging}"

docs.title: Production values.yaml
docs.text:
# helm/values.yaml (production overrides)
replicaCount: 1

image:
  repository: kairo-core
  tag: "6.1.0"
  pullPolicy: IfNotPresent

service:
  type: ClusterIP
  port: 8080
  grpcPort: 9090

storage:
  size: 50Gi
  storageClass: "standard"

auth:
  jwtSecret: ""  # set via --set or KAIRO_JWT_SECRET env var

actions:
  executionMode: suggest
  safety:
    rateLimitPerHour: 6
    cooldownSeconds: 300
    namespaceAllowlist:
      - production
      - staging

ensemble:
  adaptive: true

resources:
  requests:
    memory: "128Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "1000m"

docs.title: KairoInstance Operator
docs.text:
If you prefer a declarative CRD-based approach:

apiVersion: kairo.io/v1alpha1
kind: KairoInstance
metadata:
  name: production
  namespace: kairo-system
spec:
  image: kairo-core:6.1.0
  port: 8080
  storageSize: 50Gi
  apiKey:
    secretRef: kairo-api-key
  replicas: 1

# Create the API key secret first
kubectl create secret generic kairo-api-key \
  --from-literal=key=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \
  -n kairo-system

kubectl apply -f kairo-instance.yaml

docs.title: Docker Compose (single-host production)
docs.text:
# docker-compose.prod.yml
version: "3.8"
services:
  kairo:
    image: kairo-core:6.1.0
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
      - "9090:9090"
    volumes:
      - kairo-data:/var/lib/kairo
      - ./kairo.yaml:/etc/kairo/kairo.yaml:ro
    environment:
      KAIRO_JWT_SECRET: "${KAIRO_JWT_SECRET}"
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:8080/api/v2/health"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3

volumes:
  kairo-data:

KAIRO_JWT_SECRET=$(openssl rand -hex 32) docker compose -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d

docs.title: Prometheus integration
docs.text:
Add Kairo as a scrape target:

# prometheus.yml
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: kairo-core
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["kairo-core:8080"]
    metrics_path: /api/v2/metrics
    bearer_token: "<api-key>"

remote_write:
  - url: http://kairo-core:8080/api/v2/write
    authorization:
      credentials: "<api-key>"

docs.title: Alertmanager rules
docs.text:
# kairo-alerts.yml
groups:
  - name: kairo
    rules:
      - alert: KairoRuptureCritical
        expr: kairo_rupture_index > 3.0
        for: 2m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Rupture Index critical on {{ $labels.host }}"
          description: "R={{ $value | printf \"%.1f\" }} — check /api/v2/explain"

      - alert: KairoHealthScoreLow
        expr: kairo_kpi_healthscore < 40
        for: 5m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Health score below 40 on {{ $labels.host }}"

docs.title: Resource sizing
docs.text:
Deployment size RAM CPU Storage Edge / dev 64 MB 0.1 core 1 GB Small (< 50 hosts) 128 MB 0.25 core 10 GB Medium (50–500 hosts) 256 MB 0.5 core 30 GB Large (500+ hosts) 512 MB 1 core 100 GB

Kairo uses BadgerDB embedded — storage scales with the number of hosts and retention settings.

docs.title: Self Monitoring
docs.text: Kairo exports 14 Prometheus metric series about its own health and performance. Scrape at GET /api/v2/metrics.

docs.title: Exported metrics
docs.text: Metric Type Description kairo_rupture_index{host} Gauge Current Rupture Index per host kairo_time_to_failure_seconds{host} Gauge Predicted seconds until failure (NaN if stable) kairo_kpi_healthscore{host} Gauge Health score 0–100 per host kairo_kpi_stress{host} Gauge Stress signal 0–1 per host kairo_kpi_fatigue{host} Gauge Fatigue signal 0–1 per host kairo_kpi_pressure{host} Gauge Pressure signal 0–1 per host kairo_kpi_contagion{host} Gauge Contagion signal 0–1 per host kairo_ensemble_weight{host,model} Gauge Per-model ensemble weight (v6.1) kairo_actions_total{tier,result} Counter Actions executed by tier and outcome kairo_ingest_samples_total{source} Counter Samples ingested per source kairo_storage_bytes Gauge BadgerDB on-disk size kairo_storage_kpis_total Gauge Total KPI records stored kairo_uptime_seconds Counter Seconds since start kairo_api_requests_total{method,path,status} Counter REST API request count

docs.title: Example Grafana queries
docs.text:
# Hosts with Rupture Index above warning threshold
kairo_rupture_index > 1.5

# Average health score across all hosts
avg(kairo_kpi_healthscore)

# Ensemble weight drift — how much CA-ILR is dominating
kairo_ensemble_weight{model="ca_ilr"}

# Actions per hour (Tier-1)
rate(kairo_actions_total{tier="1"}[1h]) * 3600

# Ingest throughput
rate(kairo_ingest_samples_total[5m])

# API error rate
rate(kairo_api_requests_total{status=~"5.."}[5m])
  / rate(kairo_api_requests_total[5m])

docs.title: Prometheus scrape config
docs.text:
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: kairo-self
    scrape_interval: 15s
    static_configs:
      - targets: ["kairo-core:8080"]
    metrics_path: /api/v2/metrics
    bearer_token: "<api-key>"

docs.title: Recommended alerts
docs.text:
- alert: KairoDown
  expr: up{job="kairo-self"} == 0
  for: 1m
  labels:
    severity: critical

- alert: KairoHighActionRate
  expr: rate(kairo_actions_total{tier="1"}[10m]) * 600 > 3
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Kairo is executing Tier-1 actions at >3 per 10 min — check safety gates"

- alert: KairoStorageHigh
  expr: kairo_storage_bytes > 8e9
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Kairo BadgerDB storage approaching 10 GB limit"

docs.title: Kairo won't start
docs.text:
Symptom: Container exits immediately or logs show a startup error.

Check:

# Docker
docker logs kairo

# Kubernetes
kubectl logs -n kairo-system -l app=kairo-core --previous

Common causes:

Error message Fix jwt_secret is required Set KAIRO_JWT_SECRET env var or auth.jwt_secret in config failed to open BadgerDB Storage path not writable — check PVC/volume permissions bind: address already in use Port 8080 or 9090 already in use — change ingest.http_port or ingest.grpc_port

docs.title: Rupture Index always 0
docs.text:
Symptom: GET /api/v2/rupture/{host} returns rupture_index: 0 for all hosts.

Cause: Kairo needs at least 20 samples (5 minutes at 15 s intervals) before the burst ILR window is populated.

Fix: Wait 5–10 minutes after starting ingest, then check again.

Also check: Confirm metrics are actually being ingested:

curl http://localhost:8080/api/v2/metrics | grep kairo_ingest_samples_total
# Should be > 0

docs.title: No composite signal data
docs.text:
Symptom: GET /api/v2/kpi/stress/web-01 returns 404 or empty value.

Fix: Verify the host name matches exactly what is being ingested. Host names are case-sensitive.

# List all known hosts
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" http://localhost:8080/api/v2/ruptures | python3 -m json.tool

docs.title: Actions not firing in auto mode
docs.text:
Symptom: R > 5.0 but no Tier-1 actions are executed.

Check in order:

  1. execution_mode — must be auto, not shadow or suggest
  2. confidence_thresholds.auto_action — ensemble confidence must be ≥ 0.85
  3. namespace_allowlist — target namespace must be in the allowlist
  4. Rate limit — check kairo_actions_total{tier="1"} — if it's at rate_limit_per_hour, actions are gated
  5. Emergency stop — check if POST /api/v2/actions/emergency-stop was called
curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" http://localhost:8080/api/v2/actions

docs.title: Kafka / NATS eventbus not receiving events
docs.text:
Symptom: kairo.rupture.* topic is empty.

Check config:

# Verify eventbus driver is set
grep -A5 eventbus /etc/kairo/kairo.yaml

Check connectivity:

# NATS
nats pub kairo.test "hello" --server nats://localhost:4222

# Kafka
kafka-topics.sh --list --bootstrap-server localhost:9092

Logs:

kubectl logs -n kairo-system -l app=kairo-core | grep eventbus

docs.title: High memory usage
docs.text:
Symptom: Kairo uses much more than expected memory.

Kairo typical usage: 22 MB idle, up to ~256 MB at scale.

Causes:

  • Too many hosts — each host maintains 2 ILR windows and 8 composite signal histories
  • Retention too long — check storage.retention.kpis_days
  • BadgerDB compaction backlog — trigger manually:
curl -X POST -H "Authorization: Bearer $KEY" http://localhost:8080/api/v2/admin/compact

docs.title: gRPC ingest returning RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED
docs.text:
Symptom: gRPC clients receive RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED status.

Cause: Internal metric queue is full — the metric pipeline cannot keep up with ingest rate.

Fix:

  1. Reduce scrape interval on the sending side
  2. Increase ingest.grpc_queue_size in config (default: 10,000)
  3. Check if fusion engine is slow — look at kairo_api_requests_total latency

docs.title: Logs
docs.text:
# Kubernetes
kubectl logs -n kairo-system deployment/kairo-core --follow

# Docker
docker logs -f kairo

# Set log level to debug in config
# kairo.yaml: log_level: debug
# or env: KAIRO_LOG_LEVEL=debug

docs.title: SDKs
docs.text:
Official Kairo Core client libraries.

SDK Language Package Status Go SDK Go 1.18+ github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core/sdk/go Stable (v6.1) Python SDK Python 3.9+ kairo-client (PyPI) Stable (v6.0)

Both SDKs wrap the REST API v2 and handle auth, error decoding, and JSON unmarshalling automatically.

docs.title: Quick comparison
docs.text:
=== "Go"

```go
import ohe "github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core/sdk/go"

c := ohe.New("http://kairo-core:8080", ohe.WithAPIKey("ohe_abc123"))
health, err := c.Health(ctx)
rupture, err := c.RuptureIndex(ctx, "web-01")
weights, err := c.EnsembleWeights(ctx, "web-01")  // v6.1
```

=== "Python"

```python
from kairo import KairoClient

c = KairoClient("http://kairo-core:8080", api_key="ohe_abc123")
health = c.health()
rupture = c.rupture_index("web-01")
```

docs.title: Go SDK
docs.text: The Go SDK is part of the kairo-core module at sdk/go.

docs.title: Install
docs.text: go get github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core/sdk/go@v6.1.0

docs.title: Import
docs.text:
The package name is ohe:

import ohe "github.com/benfradjselim/kairo-core/sdk/go"

docs.title: Create a client
docs.text:
// API key auth (recommended for services)
c := ohe.New("http://kairo-core:8080", ohe.WithAPIKey("ohe_abc123"))

// JWT auth (for interactive / user sessions)
c := ohe.New("http://kairo-core:8080", ohe.WithToken("eyJ..."))

// Custom timeout
c := ohe.New("http://kairo-core:8080",
    ohe.WithAPIKey("ohe_abc123"),
    ohe.WithTimeout(10*time.Second),
)

// Multi-tenant
c := ohe.New("http://kairo-core:8080",
    ohe.WithAPIKey("ohe_abc123"),
    ohe.WithOrgID("org_xyz"),
)

docs.title: Health check
docs.text:
ctx := context.Background()

health, err := c.Health(ctx)
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
fmt.Println(health.Status)  // "ok"

docs.title: Rupture Index
docs.text:
rupture, err := c.RuptureIndex(ctx, "web-01")
if err != nil {
    log.Fatal(err)
}
fmt.Printf("R=%.2f  state=%s\n", rupture.RuptureIndex, rupture.State)

docs.title: Composite signals
docs.text:
// Single signal
kpi, err := c.KPI(ctx, "stress", "web-01")
fmt.Printf("stress=%.2f  state=%s\n", kpi.Value, kpi.State)

// Health score (0–100)
hs, err := c.KPI(ctx, "healthscore", "web-01")
fmt.Printf("healthscore=%.1f\n", hs.Value)

docs.title: Adaptive ensemble weights (v6.1)
docs.text:
weights, err := c.EnsembleWeights(ctx, "web-01")
for model, w := range weights.Weights {
    fmt.Printf("  %s: %.2f\n", model, w)
}

docs.title: Ingest metrics
docs.text:
err := c.IngestMetrics(ctx, []ohe.Metric{
    {Name: "cpu_usage", Value: 0.72, Host: "web-01", Timestamp: time.Now()},
    {Name: "mem_usage", Value: 0.45, Host: "web-01", Timestamp: time.Now()},
})

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
// List pending actions
actions, err := c.ListActions(ctx)

// Approve a suggested action
err = c.ApproveAction(ctx, "act_abc")

// Emergency stop
err = c.EmergencyStop(ctx)

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text:
The SDK returns *ohe.Error for non-2xx responses:

rupture, err := c.RuptureIndex(ctx, "unknown-host")
if err != nil {
    var apiErr *ohe.Error
    if errors.As(err, &apiErr) {
        fmt.Printf("HTTP %d: %s\n", apiErr.StatusCode, apiErr.Message)
    }
}

docs.title: Options reference
docs.text: Option Description WithAPIKey(key string) Set API key (ohe_* format) WithToken(token string) Set JWT bearer token WithOrgID(id string) Set X-Org-ID header for multi-tenancy WithTimeout(d time.Duration) HTTP request timeout (default 30s) WithHTTPClient(hc *http.Client) Replace the default HTTP client

docs.title: Python SDK
docs.text: The Python SDK is published as kairo-client on PyPI.

docs.title: Install
docs.text:
pip install kairo-client

Requires Python 3.9+. The only runtime dependency is requests>=2.28.

docs.title: Create a client
docs.text:
from kairo import KairoClient

# API key auth (recommended for services)
c = KairoClient("http://kairo-core:8080", api_key="ohe_abc123")

# Custom timeout
c = KairoClient("http://kairo-core:8080", api_key="ohe_abc123", timeout=10.0)

docs.title: Health check
docs.text:
health = c.health()
print(health["status"])   # "ok"

docs.title: Rupture Index
docs.text:
rupture = c.rupture_index("web-01")
print(f"R={rupture['rupture_index']:.2f}  state={rupture['state']}")

docs.title: Composite signals
docs.text:
# Any of: stress, fatigue, pressure, contagion,
#         resilience, entropy, sentiment, healthscore
kpi = c.kpi("stress", "web-01")
print(f"stress={kpi['value']:.2f}  state={kpi['state']}")

hs = c.kpi("healthscore", "web-01")
print(f"healthscore={hs['value']:.1f}")

docs.title: All ruptures
docs.text:
ruptures = c.ruptures()
for r in ruptures:
    print(r["host"], r["state"], r["rupture_index"])

docs.title: Ingest metrics
docs.text:
c.ingest_metrics([
    {"name": "cpu_usage", "value": 0.72, "host": "web-01"},
    {"name": "mem_usage", "value": 0.45, "host": "web-01"},
])

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
# List pending actions
actions = c.list_actions()

# Approve
c.approve_action("act_abc")

# Emergency stop
c.emergency_stop()

docs.title: Error handling
docs.text:
from kairo.exceptions import KairoError

try:
    rupture = c.rupture_index("unknown-host")
except KairoError as e:
    print(f"HTTP {e.status_code}: {e}")

docs.title: Async usage
docs.text:
The client is synchronous (requests-based). For async usage, wrap calls with asyncio.to_thread:

import asyncio
from kairo import KairoClient

c = KairoClient("http://kairo-core:8080", api_key="ohe_abc123")

async def get_rupture(host: str):
    return await asyncio.to_thread(c.rupture_index, host)

docs.title: Client reference
docs.text: Method Description health() Server health status ready() Readiness probe (raises on not-ready) rupture_index(host) Rupture Index for a host ruptures() All active ruptures kpi(signal, host) Composite signal value ensemble_weights(host) Model weights (v6.1) ingest_metrics(metrics) Push raw metrics list_actions() Pending / recent actions approve_action(id) Approve a Tier-2 action emergency_stop() Halt all Tier-1 auto-actions
